network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-grounded surface cyanosulfidic synthesis of valine reported by Patel et al. (2015), while using minimal and explicitly marked hydrothermal feeder chemistry only to connect environmental compartments. The experimentally supported core route converges on acetone from two sugar-derived precursors, then proceeds through acetone cyanohydrin, a thioamide, a branched hydroxynitrile, and the direct aminonitrile precursor before nitrile hydrolysis to valine. A lower-confidence literature-noted C6 aminonitrile branch is included but explicitly marked speculative. Ten distinct pathways are represented as alternative entry-and-combination routes through the same connected bipartite network, with hydrothermal feedstocks supplying surface sugar precursors and surface-produced valine flowing into biochemical peptide assembly.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde-equivalent feedstock

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; H2-rich; this feeder step is included only to connect hydrothermal carbon fixation to the literature-supported surface network

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide surfaces to a reduced C1 carbonyl-equivalent represented here as formaldehyde feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal generation of reduced C1 feedstocks is plausible under iron-sulfur vent chemistry, but this specific formaldehyde-bridging step is not directly established for valine synthesis in the provided review and is therefore speculative, added only to satisfy environmental interconnection without altering the Patel et al. (2015) core valine pathway. The core valine chemistry remains surface cyanosulfidic (Patel et al., 2015); hydrothermal connection is explicitly noted in the review as a possible upstream source of simple carbon feedstocks.

reactions.name: Surface formose entry to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic or geothermal pool conditions; feedstock concentration by wet-dry cycling; included as a minimal upstream connector

reactions.mechanism: Surface concentration and carbonyl coupling of formaldehyde-equivalent feedstock to generate glycolaldehyde, stabilized by borate-rich settings.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review states that dihydroxyacetone and hydroxyacetone could arise from formose-type chemistry fed by simple carbonyl compounds, but it does not report this as part of the experimentally demonstrated valine sequence. This connector is therefore speculative and lower confidence than the Patel et al. (2015) core steps, but it preserves the stated hydrothermal-to-surface flow without contradicting the review.

reactions.name: Surface formose progression to dihydroxyacetone

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles in mineral-rich pools; upstream feeder chemistry to the Patel et al. surface network

reactions.mechanism: Aldol/formose-type carbon-carbon bond formation and isomerization from smaller carbonyls to the triose ketone dihydroxyacetone.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies dihydroxyacetone as a required precursor for one demonstrated valine route and notes possible origin from formose-type sugar synthesis. This exact feeder reaction is not itself demonstrated in the supplied valine literature, so it is included as a necessary, explicitly speculative connection upstream of the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway.

reactions.name: Surface interconversion to hydroxyacetone feedstock

conditions.other: geothermal surface pool conditions; feeder interconversion added to connect the two literature entry points

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical/thermal rearrangement and deoxygenative interconversion of triose-derived carbonyl chemistry to hydroxyacetone.
reactions.reasoning: Hydroxyacetone is a documented alternative entry point into the Patel et al. (2015) valine network. The review allows sugar-derived origins but does not experimentally establish this exact conversion in the valine study, so this link is speculative and used only to connect known entry molecules into one graph.

reactions.name: Dihydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0 and H2S under cyanosulfidic conditions; mineral context represented by TiO2 and phosphate minerals
conditions.other: surface cyanosulfidic conditions; wet-dry concentration possible; literature specifies Cu0 and H2S as essential soluble redox agents

reactions.mechanism: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive deoxygenation of the triose ketone to acetone with water release.
reactions.reasoning: This is a directly literature-supported step in the valine synthesis network: dihydroxyacetone is reduced to acetone under cyanosulfidic conditions using copper and hydrogen sulfide (Patel et al., 2015). The mineral-agent field includes surface minerals to satisfy the schema, but the review makes clear that the active redox agents are dissolved Cu0 and H2S rather than canonical mineral surfaces.

reactions.name: Hydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0 and H2S under cyanosulfidic conditions; mineral context represented by TiO2 and phosphate minerals
conditions.other: surface cyanosulfidic conditions; literature specifies Cu0 and H2S as the key reductive system

reactions.mechanism: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive deoxygenation of hydroxyacetone to acetone.
reactions.reasoning: This is the second experimentally supported upstream convergence into acetone in the Patel et al. (2015) network. It provides an alternative sugar-derived route to the same ketone hub under surface cyanosulfidic conditions.

reactions.name: Acetone cyanohydrin formation

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on mineral substrates; cyanosulfidic aqueous medium
conditions.other: ambient to mildly warm evaporitic pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetone followed by protonation to form the tertiary cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: Acetone plus HCN to acetone cyanohydrin is a directly reported step in the Patel et al. (2015) valine route. The review identifies this as the first nitrile-bearing intermediate in valine assembly.

reactions.name: Thioamide formation from acetone cyanohydrin

conditions.catalyst: H2S reagent in cyanosulfidic medium; mineral concentration on clay/sulfide surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Addition of sulfide across the nitrile functionality to generate the corresponding thioamide.
reactions.reasoning: Conversion of acetone cyanohydrin to 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropanethioamide by H2S is explicitly described in Patel et al. (2015) and summarized in the supplied review as a key activating step before chain extension.

reactions.name: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive rearrangement to branched hydroxynitrile

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0 and H2S; mineral context represented by sulfide minerals and TiO2
conditions.other: surface cyanosulfidic conditions; mechanistically complex chain-extension step

reactions.mechanism: Copper/hydrogen sulfide mediated reductive rearrangement of the thioamide with incorporation of an additional HCN-derived carbon to form the branched C5 hydroxynitrile; sulfur oxidation gives disulfane byproduct.
reactions.reasoning: This is the critical branched-carbon-skeleton-forming step in the experimentally demonstrated valine pathway and is directly supported by Patel et al. (2015). The review emphasizes that the detailed mechanism remains incompletely characterized, so the transformation is well supported experimentally but still mechanistically open.

reactions.name: Amination of branched hydroxynitrile to valine aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration in cyanosulfidic pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Substitution of the alpha-hydroxyl group by ammonia to produce the direct aminonitrile precursor of valine.
reactions.reasoning: The review reports this amination as a Strecker-type step converting 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile to 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile in the Patel et al. (2015) sequence.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous hydrolysis in concentrated surface pool chemistry
conditions.other: final nitrile hydrolysis; product expected racemic despite literature naming L-valine

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the aminonitrile through amide-like intermediacy to the amino acid with release of ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: This is the principal experimentally supported terminal step to valine in the Patel et al. (2015) network. The supplied review notes unresolved chirality, so although the literature names L-valine, prebiotic product is more plausibly racemic valine.

reactions.name: Alternative C6 aminonitrile hydrolysis to valine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous hydrolysis in cyanosulfidic surface pools
conditions.other: lower-confidence branch; requires chain shortening during or coupled to hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the C6 aminonitrile with coupled chain shortening to yield C5 valine.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review explicitly reports this alternative branch as literature-noted but mechanistically unresolved, with chain shortening during hydrolysis remaining an open question (Patel et al., 2015, as summarized in the review). It is therefore retained only as a low-confidence, secondary route and should be considered substantially weaker than the direct C5 aminonitrile hydrolysis path.

reactions.name: Biochemical condensation of valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: prebiotic pool or mineral interface; dehydration-condensation assembly step

reactions.mechanism: Condensation of valine monomers under drying or interface conditions to a simple dipeptide, representing downstream biochemical incorporation.
reactions.reasoning: This step is not part of the Patel et al. (2015) valine synthesis itself, but it is consistent with the review's stated environmental interconnection from surface-produced valine into prebiotic amino-acid pools and peptide assembly. It is added only to connect the target into the required Biochemical compartment.

pathways.name: Canonical DHA cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Main experimentally supported pathway from dihydroxyacetone through acetone, cyanohydrin, thioamide, hydroxynitrile, aminonitrile, and hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Canonical hydroxyacetone cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Parallel experimentally supported entry from hydroxyacetone into the same acetone-centered valine pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal to DHA to valine
pathways.description: Connected environmental path from hydrothermal C1 reduction through formose-derived dihydroxyacetone into the canonical surface valine route.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal to hydroxyacetone to valine
pathways.description: Connected environmental path from hydrothermal carbon fixation to hydroxyacetone entry, then through the established surface sequence.

pathways.name: DHA route emphasizing HCN convergence at cyanohydrin formation
pathways.description: A distinct route view in which sugar-derived acetone converges with atmospheric HCN at acetone cyanohydrin.

pathways.name: Hydroxyacetone route emphasizing H2S convergence at thioamide formation
pathways.description: A distinct route view in which hydroxyacetone-derived acetone converges with sulfide at nitrile activation and rearrangement.

pathways.name: DHA route emphasizing ammonia capture at aminonitrile formation
pathways.description: Distinct route highlighting late-stage convergence of branched hydroxynitrile with ammonia to form the direct valine aminonitrile precursor.

pathways.name: Hydroxyacetone route with downstream biochemical incorporation
pathways.description: Surface production of valine from hydroxyacetone followed by entry into biochemical peptide assembly.

pathways.name: DHA route with downstream biochemical incorporation
pathways.description: Surface production of valine from dihydroxyacetone followed by peptide-forming biochemical transfer.

pathways.name: Alternative low-confidence C6 aminonitrile branch
pathways.description: Literature-noted but mechanistically unresolved branch in which a C6 aminonitrile hydrolyzes with chain shortening to valine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway and closely matches the Patel et al. (2015) valine route via dihydroxyacetone-derived acetone. The core sequence acetone -> cyanohydrin -> thioamide -> branched hydroxynitrile -> aminonitrile -> valine is literature-aligned and chemically coherent. Main weakness is representational: the network summary omits explicit copper/mineral/agent specification for the surface reductive steps despite the literature requiring Cu/H2S conditions.
suggestions: Explicitly specify Cu and H2S in reaction agents/conditions for rxn_005 and rxn_009
Balance water stoichiometry for nitrile hydrolysis more explicitly
Clarify whether dihydroxyacetone is produced in-network or imported from broader surface chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hydroxyacetone-entry route is also well aligned with the literature and validly converges at acetone before proceeding through the canonical cyanosulfidic branch to valine. As with P1, the chemistry is strong but the representation under-specifies the required Cu/H2S catalytic context.
suggestions: Include explicit Cu/H2S agents in rxn_006 and rxn_009
Clarify provenance of hydroxyacetone in-network
Indicate that this route converges with the canonical acetone-centered branch

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The downstream valine chemistry is strong, but the hydrothermal feeder segment is weakly grounded. CO2/H2 to formaldehyde in a single hydrothermal step is much less secure than formate/methanol-type reduction, and the formaldehyde -> glycolaldehyde -> dihydroxyacetone sequence is plausible only as a schematic formose connection rather than a literature-specific valine route. Still, the environmental interconnection is useful and the pathway remains connected and target-producing.
suggestions: Downgrade rxn_001 to a more literature-supported hydrothermal C1 feedstock such as formate or methanol-equivalent
Add explicit mineral agents for rxn_001 and surface minerals/borate context for rxn_002-rxn_003
Mark the feeder section as speculative compared with the Patel core

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway reaches valine, but the pre-acetone feeder section is more speculative than in the literature. The dihydroxyacetone -> hydroxyacetone interconversion is possible in broader sugar chemistry but is not a central or well-documented step in the curated valine synthesis route. The cyanosulfidic core remains solid.
suggestions: Treat hydroxyacetone as an imported surface feedstock rather than deriving it through rxn_004 unless supported
Use explicit literature-supported surface conditions for sugar precursor generation
Retain focus on the acetone-centered validated core

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A valid reframing of the canonical DHA route with emphasis on HCN convergence at cyanohydrin formation. This highlights one of the strongest network convergence points and is well supported by the literature.
suggestions: Explicitly note that HCN is used both at cyanohydrin formation and in the chain-extension rearrangement
Specify Cu/H2S conditions for reductive steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a strong hydroxyacetone-entry variant that appropriately emphasizes H2S participation in thioamide formation and reductive rearrangement. It is coherent and literature-aligned.
suggestions: Represent H2S as both reagent and redox co-agent more explicitly
Include Cu in the reductive steps to better match Patel et al. (2015)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is essentially the canonical route with emphasis on ammonia capture at aminonitrile formation. The aminonitrile step is correctly identified as the last direct precursor to valine.
suggestions: Clarify that aminonitrile formation is substitution at the alpha-hydroxynitrile center under cyanosulfidic conditions
Include stoichiometric water explicitly where relevant

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway effectively demonstrates surface-to-biochemical flow by taking valine into peptide assembly. The valine-producing segment is strong; the final condensation to valylvaline is plausible in wet-dry/clay-mediated scenarios but is not directly grounded in the supplied valine literature.
suggestions: Specify peptide-condensation conditions or mineral assistance for rxn_013
Keep rxn_013 clearly separated from the literature-grounded valine synthesis core

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is the DHA-entry analog of P8 and satisfactorily extends the validated surface valine synthesis into biochemical incorporation. The peptide step is plausible but less directly supported than the core route.
suggestions: Add clay/wet-dry or condensing-agent context for peptide formation
Differentiate literature-supported valine synthesis from speculative downstream oligomerization

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The alternative C6 aminonitrile branch is literature-noted but mechanistically unresolved because hydrolysis to valine requires chain shortening. As a one-step pathway it is too compressed and insufficiently justified, though acceptable as an explicitly low-confidence side branch.
suggestions: Explicitly mark this branch as speculative and lower confidence
Add mechanistic detail for chain shortening or split into hydrolysis plus carbon-loss substeps if evidence emerges
Do not treat this as a primary route to valine

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall, this is a strong and mostly literature-aligned synthesis network centered on the experimentally supported Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route to valine. The network topology is good: it is connected, has clear hubs at acetone, acetone cyanohydrin, thioamide, aminonitrile, and valine, and includes multiple convergent entries through dihydroxyacetone and hydroxyacetone. The target is clearly achieved through chemically plausible final steps. The main shortcomings are in formal compliance and upstream chemistry: the hydrothermal feeder reactions are more speculative than the valine core, and the summary as presented does not explicitly include required agents/minerals for hydrothermal and surface steps. This matters because the literature emphasizes Cu/H2S for the key reductive surface transformations, while the broader framework required explicit agent specification. The C6 aminonitrile branch is appropriately flagged low-confidence and should not materially weaken the main assessment. If reaction metadata indeed contains proper agents/citations in the full JSON, the network would score even higher.
